nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers, floodplains, and associated lakes with sandy or muddy substrates; the Nile River and its tributaries, as well as other major West and East African river systems including the Niger, Volta, Gambia, and Geba basins

decorPreferences: sandy substrate for resting and hunting
large smooth rocks and caves for shelter and territory
driftwood as cover and to break sightlines
minimal or no planting (will uproot and destroy plants)
robust canister filtration with high turnover (10x volume/hour recommended)

diet.notes: Obligate carnivore requiring hard-shelled prey to wear down continuously-growing fused beak teeth. Core diet: live or fresh snails (ramshorn, Malaysian trumpet, pond snails), whole unshelled prawns/shrimp, crayfish, mussels in shell, clams, and cockles. Supplemented with meaty foods such as earthworms, tilapia fillet, and whole fish. Hard-shelled food is not optional — without regular beak wear, teeth overgrow and the fish cannot eat. Pellets and dried foods are generally refused; train young specimens onto varied whole foods early. Feed 2–3 times weekly; avoid overfeeding as this species is prone to obesity.

avoidWith: all other fish species (will attack and kill any tankmate)
conspecifics (extreme intraspecific aggression; must be kept alone)
any fish with fins (compulsive fin-nipper; attacks within minutes)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Tetraodon lineatus; shows characteristic striped pattern on adult specimen.

summary: The largest true freshwater pufferfish, reaching 40–45 cm. An intensely aggressive African species requiring a species-only setup. Continuously-growing beak teeth must be worn down with hard-shelled prey (snails, crustaceans). Advanced care only.
careNotes:
The Fahaka Puffer is the largest strictly freshwater pufferfish in the world, native to the Nile River basin and other major African river systems including the Niger, Volta, and Gambia. Adults commonly reach 38–45 cm and are powerfully built, with an unmistakable yellow-and-black striped lateral pattern on a cream body. A tank of at least 500 L (ideally 700 L+) with a footprint of 180 cm or more is required for a single adult; no tankmates of any kind are appropriate. This species is not merely aggressive — it will relentlessly attack, fin-nip, and kill any companion, including conspecifics. It must be kept in strict isolation for life.

The most critical husbandry point is dental management. Like all puffers, Tetraodon lineatus has fused beak-like teeth that grow continuously throughout its life. Without regular abrasion from hard-shelled prey, the beak overgrows and the fish becomes unable to eat — a common cause of premature death in captivity. The staple diet must consist of appropriately-sized live or fresh snails (ramshorn, Malaysian trumpet, pond snails), whole unshelled prawns, crayfish, mussels and clams in shell, and similar hard foods. Soft foods alone are inadequate. A diverse rotation of hard-shelled prey is ideal.

Filtration must be exceptionally strong — a high-protein messy carnivore diet in a large tank demands canister filtration rated at 8–10x tank volume per hour, along with regular large water changes (30–50% weekly). Ammonia and nitrite must be kept at zero; elevated nitrates stress the species and contribute to skin ulcers. Tetraodon lineatus is sensitive to deteriorating water quality and will exhibit colour fading, reduced appetite, and surface-gasping as early warning signs.

Fahaka Puffers are highly intelligent and interactive; many individuals recognise their keeper and beg actively at feeding time. However, handlers must exercise caution — the beak can deliver a severe bite capable of removing finger tissue, and the fish will not hesitate to bite a hand in the tank. Use a feeding stick or tongs for all feeding interactions.

Note on toxicity: Like many puffers, Tetraodon lineatus may carry tetrodotoxin (TTX) in its organs, though this is not relevant under normal aquarium conditions (TTX is not released into the water and does not threaten tankmates). It is distinct from the ostracitoxin/pahutoxin skin-slime toxin released by boxfishes; fahaka puffers do not release tank-toxic slime under stress.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare and has not been reliably documented in home aquaria. In the wild, Tetraodon lineatus is known to undertake seasonal upstream migrations for spawning during flood seasons. The extreme aggression between conspecifics — even between potential mates — makes captive pairing attempts highly dangerous to both fish. No reproducible captive breeding protocol is established; this species is considered not breedable in captivity under standard aquarium conditions.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tetraodon lineatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Adult size to 43 cm, temperature 24–28°C, pH 6.5–8.0, hardness 5–20 dH, native range across African river systems, diet (hard-shelled invertebrates essential for beak wear), extreme aggression, solitary keeping requirement, strong filtration demand, and captive breeding notes all derived or confirmed here.

additional.name: FishBase — Tetraodon lineatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (45 cm TL), native range countries, taxonomy (Tetraodontidae, Tetraodontiformes), habitat (large rivers and floodplains in the Nile, Niger, Volta, Gambia, and Geba basins), and depth/substrate data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pea Puffer and Puffer Fish Care
additional.notes: Supplementary source for general puffer husbandry principles: beak-overgrowth risk, hard-shelled diet requirement, conspecific and community aggression, water quality sensitivity, and feeding behaviour. Fahaka-specific data confirmed against SeriouslyFish and FishBase.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Fahaka Puffer species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed species-only requirement, minimum tank size guidance (300 gal / ~1,100 L for adult cited in trade; 500 L used as minimum for younger specimens), diet diversity, beak management, bite risk to handlers, and that captive breeding is not established.

additional.name: Pufferfish.co.uk — Tetraodon lineatus care sheet
additional.notes: Specialist puffer community source. Corroborated adult size 35–45 cm, strict solitary keeping, beak-wear dietary requirements, high filtration demand, and aggressive handler interaction.

fish.breedingNotes: No reproducible captive breeding protocol exists. Extreme conspecific aggression makes pairing highly dangerous. Wild breeding involves seasonal upstream spawning migrations. Not considered achievable in home aquaria.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 40–45 cm and requires a strictly species-only tank with no viable tankmates; specialist single-fish setup only.
